title: Oxford Challenge
seoDescription: Take a fast Oxford-style reasoning quiz with 80 staged questions covering logic, patterns, evidence, numbers, and careful thinking.
pageTitle: Only 3% Can Pass This Oxford Challenge
summary: A staged 80-question challenge built around careful logic, patterns, evidence, and calm problem-solving.

homepage.title: Can You Pass This Oxford Challenge?
homepage.summary: Think you can stay calm when the questions start getting seriously difficult?

footer.aboutText: This Oxford-style challenge is a staged reasoning quiz built around logical thinking, pattern recognition, careful inference, evidence evaluation, and problem-solving. The University of Oxford is one of the world's oldest universities, with teaching traced back to the 11th century, and is known worldwide for careful discussion and academic debate. This quiz borrows that sense of intellectual challenge for entertainment, curiosity, and self-challenge, not as an official University of Oxford examination or official assessment.

landing.quickStartText: Looks easy at first. Then it gets harder.

profiles.title: You thought like a strong problem solver.
profiles.copy: Exceptional reasoning across the full challenge. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Strong performance.
profiles.copy: You stayed sharp as the questions became more demanding. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Good score.
profiles.copy: You handled the core reasoning rounds well. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Keep sharpening.
profiles.copy: The later rounds reward calm pattern recognition and careful logic. Your best area was {stage}.

stageGroups.title: Scholar Warm Up
stageGroups.encouragement: Good start. The first round was about quick decisions and simple patterns. Next, the comparisons need a little more care.

questions.prompt: Which item is most useful for planning a study session?
questions.explanation: A calendar helps organise dates and times for study.

questions.prompt: What is 7 + 2?
questions.explanation: 7 plus 2 equals 9.

questions.prompt: A note says, “Read the question before answering.” What should you do first?
questions.explanation: The instruction says to read the question before answering.

questions.prompt: Which shape has no corners?
questions.explanation: A circle has a curved edge and no corners.

questions.prompt: What comes next: 2, 4, 8, 16, ____?
questions.explanation: Each number is doubled, so 16 becomes 32.

questions.prompt: All badges are tokens. This object is a badge. What must be true?
questions.explanation: If every badge is a token, then this badge must be a token.

questions.prompt: Which pair shows the strongest contrast?
questions.explanation: Full and empty are opposites; the other pairs are similar.

questions.prompt: A desk is too dark for reading. Which action is most direct?
questions.explanation: Turning on a desk light directly increases light for reading.

stageGroups.title: Careful Comparisons
stageGroups.encouragement: Nice work. Now the questions ask you to compare information more carefully and avoid simple mistakes.

questions.prompt: Which number is the smallest?
questions.explanation: 0.24 is smaller than 0.33, 0.40, and 0.42.

questions.prompt: A report says errors became less common, but did not disappear. Which summary is accurate?
questions.explanation: Less common means reduced, not removed completely.

questions.prompt: Which list is in order from shortest to longest?
questions.explanation: A minute is shorter than an hour, and an hour is shorter than a day.

questions.prompt: Which rule best completes the pattern: small, medium, large, small, medium, ____?
questions.explanation: The cycle is small, medium, large, then it repeats.

questions.prompt: If all clear answers give a reason, and this answer gives no reason, what can you conclude?
choices: It is not a clear answer
questions.explanation: If clear answers always give a reason, an answer without a reason is not clear by that rule.

questions.prompt: Which option is the best example of evidence?
questions.explanation: A measured result is evidence because it gives information that can support a claim.

questions.prompt: Which set contains exactly four items?
questions.explanation: The first set lists four items.

questions.prompt: A plan saves time but increases mistakes. What is the fairest conclusion?
questions.explanation: Saving time while increasing mistakes creates a trade-off.

stageGroups.title: Pattern Signals
stageGroups.encouragement: You are moving well. The next round turns simple patterns into rules you need to notice quickly.

questions.prompt: Which number completes the sequence: 3, 6, 9, 12, ____?
questions.explanation: The sequence adds 3 each time, so the next number is 15.

questions.prompt: A code changes 4 to 8 and 7 to 14. What does it change 9 to?
questions.explanation: The rule doubles the number, so 9 becomes 18.

questions.prompt: Which statement is a cautious conclusion?
questions.explanation: A cautious conclusion avoids claiming more than the evidence supports.

questions.prompt: All green cards are valid. This card is green. What must be true?
questions.explanation: The rule says every green card is valid, so this green card is valid.

questions.prompt: Which option breaks the pattern: circle, square, circle, square, triangle?
questions.explanation: The pattern alternates circle and square, so triangle breaks it.

questions.prompt: A box has 10 markers. Four are removed. How many remain?
questions.explanation: 10 minus 4 equals 6.

questions.prompt: Which question best checks whether a claim is supported?
questions.explanation: A supported claim needs evidence that backs it.

questions.prompt: A route is blocked. Which response shows flexible thinking?
questions.explanation: Looking for another route adapts to the problem.

stageGroups.title: Evidence Check
stageGroups.encouragement: Good reasoning. This round tested support and evidence. Next, the numbers become more connected.

questions.prompt: A study uses only 6 people. What is the best concern?
choices: The sample may be too small
questions.explanation: A very small sample may not represent a wider group well.

questions.prompt: Which result best supports the claim that practice improved speed?
choices: The task had a long title
The same task was completed faster after practice
questions.explanation: Completing the same task faster after practice directly supports the claim.

questions.prompt: What is 25% of 40?
questions.explanation: 25% is one quarter, and one quarter of 40 is 10.

questions.prompt: Which sentence gives a cause and effect relationship?
choices: Because the floor was wet, the sign was placed nearby
The sign was yellow and tall
The floor was near the door
questions.explanation: The sentence links the wet floor as the reason for placing the sign.

questions.prompt: Which item is the odd one out if the rule is “used to measure”?
questions.explanation: A blank card is not normally a measuring tool.

questions.prompt: If no silver tokens are heavy, and this token is silver, what must be true?
questions.explanation: The rule says silver tokens are not heavy.

questions.prompt: A chart rises from left to right. What does that usually show?
questions.explanation: A line that rises from left to right usually shows an increase.

questions.prompt: Which action would most improve a weak explanation?
questions.explanation: A clear reason helps explain why the conclusion follows.

stageGroups.title: Number Balance
stageGroups.encouragement: That was solid. Now the test asks you to balance quantities, rules, and short deductions at the same time.

questions.prompt: A value rises from 20 to 30. What is the increase?
questions.explanation: 30 minus 20 equals an increase of 10.

questions.prompt: Which ratio is equivalent to 6:9?
questions.explanation: Both 6 and 9 divide by 3, giving 2:3.

questions.prompt: A rule says only approved files can be opened. A file opens. What must be true?
questions.explanation: If only approved files can open, an opened file must be approved.

questions.prompt: What comes next: 5, 8, 12, 17, ____?
questions.explanation: The increases are 3, 4, and 5, so the next increase is 6, giving 23.

questions.prompt: Which comparison is fairest?
choices: Compare one full week with one hour
Compare two results measured in different units
Compare the same measure over the same period
questions.explanation: A fair comparison uses the same measure over the same period.

questions.prompt: A table lists four scores: 6, 7, 7, 8. What is the average?
questions.explanation: The total is 28, and 28 divided by 4 is 7.

questions.prompt: Which detail is most important when following a deadline?
questions.explanation: A deadline depends on the required date.

questions.prompt: A solution works for one example only. What should you check next?
choices: Whether it has a short name
Whether it works in more cases
questions.explanation: A strong solution should be tested beyond one example.

stageGroups.title: Logical Conditions
stageGroups.encouragement: You are past the easy middle. The next stage is more conditional: rules, limits, and what really follows.

questions.prompt: If a number is divisible by 10, it ends in 0. Which number fits?
questions.explanation: 80 ends in 0, so it is divisible by 10.

questions.prompt: All rare coins in a tray are labelled. This coin is rare and in the tray. What follows?
questions.explanation: The rule says every rare coin in the tray is labelled.

questions.prompt: Which claim contains an unsupported assumption?
choices: A longer meeting always creates a better decision
questions.explanation: It makes a broad claim that would need support.

questions.prompt: What number completes the pattern: 1, 4, 9, 16, ____?
questions.explanation: These are square numbers: 1, 4, 9, 16, then 25.

questions.prompt: A message says “Submit after review.” Which action follows the message?
questions.explanation: The message requires review before submission.

questions.prompt: Which result would weaken the claim that a new method caused improvement?
choices: A similar group improved without the method
questions.explanation: If a similar group improved without the method, the method may not be the cause.

questions.prompt: A cube has 6 faces. How many faces do 3 cubes have in total?
questions.explanation: 3 cubes have 3 times 6 faces, which is 18.

questions.prompt: A question seems hard because it includes extra details. What is the best strategy?
questions.explanation: Filtering relevant information helps solve complex questions.

stageGroups.title: Careful Reasoning
stageGroups.encouragement: Strong progress. This round asks you to explain, compare, and choose the strongest reason.

questions.prompt: Which answer gives the best reason?
choices: The plan is stronger because it reduces risk and keeps the main benefit
The plan is good because I like it
The plan is good because it is first
The plan is good because it sounds certain
questions.explanation: The strongest answer gives a reason based on risk and benefit.

questions.prompt: A machine triples a number and subtracts 1. If the input is 5, what is the output?
questions.explanation: 5 tripled is 15, and 15 minus 1 is 14.

questions.prompt: Which conclusion follows: Some guides are maps. All maps show routes.
questions.explanation: The guides that are maps show routes, so some guides show routes.

questions.prompt: Which pattern uses alternating rules: add 2, then multiply by 2?
questions.explanation: 2 plus 2 is 4, 4 times 2 is 8, and 8 plus 2 is 10.

questions.prompt: A speaker says a claim is true because many people repeat it. What is the weakness?
choices: Repeating a claim does not prove it
questions.explanation: Popularity or repetition does not prove truth.

questions.prompt: Which item is least like the others?
questions.explanation: A sphere is three-dimensional, while the others are flat shapes.

questions.prompt: A candidate answers calmly but gives no evidence. What is missing?
questions.explanation: Calm delivery does not replace evidence.

questions.prompt: Which response best shows intellectual flexibility?
choices: Keep the first answer no matter what
Revise the answer when stronger evidence appears
questions.explanation: Flexibility means updating a view when better evidence appears.

stageGroups.title: Assumption Tests
stageGroups.encouragement: Good work. Now the challenge asks what is being quietly assumed inside an argument.

questions.prompt: Argument: “The room is full, so the talk must be useful.” What is assumed?
choices: People attend because the talk is useful
questions.explanation: The argument assumes attendance is due to usefulness, not another reason.

questions.prompt: A fair test changes only one factor at a time. Why?
choices: To identify what caused the result
questions.explanation: Changing one factor helps identify the cause of any change.

questions.prompt: What comes next: 2, 5, 11, 23, ____?
questions.explanation: Each term doubles and adds 1: 2, 5, 11, 23, then 47.

questions.prompt: A price rises by 20% from 50. What is the new price?
questions.explanation: 20% of 50 is 10, so the new price is 60.

questions.prompt: Which evidence is strongest for comparing two methods?
choices: One method has a shorter name
Both methods tested under similar conditions
questions.explanation: Similar conditions make the comparison fairer.

questions.prompt: If every safe bridge passed inspection, and this bridge did not pass inspection, what follows?
questions.explanation: If every safe bridge passed inspection, then a bridge that did not pass inspection is not safe by that rule.

questions.prompt: Which row would complete an increasing pattern of 2, 4, 6 items?
questions.explanation: The count increases by 2, so after 2, 4, and 6 comes 8.

questions.prompt: A problem asks for the “best” option but gives several good choices. What should you do?
choices: Compare the choices against the stated goal
questions.explanation: The best option depends on the goal given in the question.

stageGroups.title: Layered Reasoning
stageGroups.encouragement: Excellent. The final stretch now combines numbers, conditions, and careful reasoning.

questions.prompt: A group has 4 red tokens, 3 blue tokens, and 5 green tokens. What fraction are blue?
questions.explanation: There are 12 tokens total and 3 are blue, so the fraction is 3/12, or 1/4.

questions.prompt: Which conclusion is safest: People who trained more scored higher, but no starting scores were recorded?
choices: Training may be linked to higher scores, but starting ability could matter
questions.explanation: Without starting scores, it is hard to know whether training caused the difference.

questions.prompt: All precise reports include units. This report has no units. What follows?
choices: It is not precise by that rule
questions.explanation: If precise reports must include units, a report without units is not precise by the rule.

questions.prompt: What number completes the pattern: 1, 2, 6, 24, ____?
questions.explanation: The pattern multiplies by 2, then 3, then 4, so next is times 5, giving 120.

questions.prompt: A decision lowers cost but reduces quality. What should be considered?
choices: The trade-off between cost and quality
questions.explanation: Both the saving and the quality loss matter.

questions.prompt: A set has values 3, 4, 4, 5, 24. Which measure better resists the extreme value?
questions.explanation: The median is less affected by the unusually large value 24.

questions.prompt: Which statement best defines a counterexample?
choices: A case that challenges a general rule
questions.explanation: A counterexample is a case that shows a general rule may not always hold.

questions.prompt: A diagram shows A above B, B above C, and C above D. Which is second from the top?
questions.explanation: The order from top to bottom is A, B, C, D, so B is second.

stageGroups.title: Final 3% Challenge
stageGroups.encouragement: One final round. These questions reward calm reading and careful inference more than speed.

questions.prompt: A rule says: if a claim is proven, it has evidence. Which statement is equivalent?
choices: If a claim has evidence, it is proven
If a claim has no evidence, it is not proven
If a claim is popular, it is proven
If a claim is not proven, it has evidence
questions.explanation: The statement with no evidence cannot be proven follows from the original rule.

questions.prompt: A fair coin is flipped twice. What is the chance of getting two tails?
questions.explanation: The four equally likely outcomes are HH, HT, TH, and TT, so TT is 1 in 4.

questions.prompt: Which objection is strongest: “No one objected, so everyone agreed”?
questions.explanation: Silence does not necessarily mean agreement.

questions.prompt: What comes next: 4, 6, 10, 18, 34, ____?
questions.explanation: The increases are 2, 4, 8, and 16, so the next increase is 32, giving 66.

questions.prompt: All complete solutions are checked. Some checked solutions are revised. What must be true?
questions.explanation: The first statement directly says all complete solutions are checked; nothing definite follows about revision.

questions.prompt: Two explanations fit the same evidence, but one uses fewer unsupported assumptions. Which is usually stronger?
choices: The one with the longer title
The one with fewer unsupported assumptions
The one with more examples of style
questions.explanation: When evidence is equally explained, fewer unsupported assumptions usually make an explanation stronger.

questions.prompt: A puzzle includes many details, but only two affect the result. What is the best move?
questions.explanation: Difficult questions often require separating relevant details from distractions.

questions.prompt: A number is increased by 50% and becomes 90. What was the original number?
questions.explanation: If the original is increased by 50%, it becomes 1.5 times the original. 90 divided by 1.5 is 60.
